based_on: mined directly from a real transcript at schema v2 (no v1 intermediate)

session.title: skills triage becomes the crucible: design, build, prove by recursion, ship

intent.text: Prove crucible works and ship it: test-and-approve the skills autonomously, benchmark model×effort, package the plugin, and get the real thing installed from the marketplace.

history.text: Get the standalone skills reviewed and ranked by ROI — they were written hurriedly and are probably below standard.
history.superseded_because: Pi triaged the review's findings live: three skills matter day-to-day, three he can live without — the goal narrowed from ranking to repair.

history.text: Bring worktree, red-vs-blue, and hats up to latest standards: fix worktree for this Mac, and ground red-vs-blue + hats in documented research; brief/subset/audit are parked.
history.superseded_because: The design conversation outgrew two skill rewrites: Pi asked for one-word names and a real architecture, and 'crucible' — a plugin-shaped review system — became the object of the session.

history.text: Forge hats + red-vs-blue into crucible: a review system with a hat wardrobe, stance agents, a consult axis, and a worthiness gate — designed on research, not vibes.
history.superseded_because: Pi granted test-and-approve autonomy ('fucking see if they work or not') and pointed at a real marketplace: building gave way to proving and shipping.

frontier.detail: session closed at 03:41 — one live check deliberately left open: whether the plan-mode hook matcher actually fires (transcript line 1975)

compaction_events.note: /compact at transcript line 1528 (02:57); the SHIP line opens from the compacted summary at line 1540

lines.label: Which of these skills deserve to live?

because.text: I want to start work on the skills present in this repo, I believe they were written in an hurried manner due to which they are pretty shit. Please do a check on the standalone skills present in the @skills/ folder. Out of them group them by best ROI.
because.speaker: user

lines.outcome: Answered at n07@1: three keepers (worktree, red-vs-blue, hats) brought to standard — worktree fixed and installed, the other two rewritten on a 108-agent research base; brief/subset/audit parked.

lines.label: What should hats + red-vs-blue become?

because.text: So let's just discuss these two skills because I feel like I wanna use them a lot. And let's, um, kind of come up with a good design and decisions on them.
because.speaker: user

lines.outcome: Answered at n13@1: crucible v1 exists end-to-end — wardrobe (10 hats + authoring contract), consult, hats, red-vs-blue, stance agents — 16 files rewritten to the research contract, all validators green, nothing committed.

segments.label: hat-file + contract research (opus orchestrating sonnet readers)

lines.label: Does crucible actually work?

because.text: like i just need you to test them and, uh, fucking see if they work or not if they work then they're approved like you don't need to wait for me
because.speaker: user

lines.outcome: Answered at n19@1, the hard way: all five skills passed conformance and shipped, the benchmark kept the pins — and the dogfood panel caught the distinctness eval rigging its own result, which the fix-and-re-run cycle survived.

lines.label: Get the real thing installed

because.text: Lets work on this now - Still open (logged, not silently dropped): silent blue-failure handling, the "hats" naming overload, hook-autoload verification (needs a live install), and whether effort: frontmatter is even honored.
because.speaker: user

lines.outcome: Done at n23@1: XSS fixed, gates added, hook/effort mechanics verified, push authorized (retiring the no-commits rule), version-gate beaten with bumps — crucible 0.2.0 and manifold 0.1.1 installed and inventoried. One live check left open (n24).

beats.title: Eight skills, three keepers
beats.note: a ~5-hour gap sits between the research landing and the rewrites

beats.title: Over the wall: commit, bump, install

artifacts.name: worktree skill — fixed for macOS + Linux, registered in the manifest, installed live
deep-research cited report — the evidence base for red-vs-blue and hats
adversarial-review methodology doc — decision register D1–D11, later D12–D17
hats + red-vs-blue SKILL.md rewritten on the research base, eval definitions added
crucible design doc — architecture, mermaid diagram, invocation model, open questions
crucible v1 — wardrobe (HATS.md contract + 10 hats), consult skill, stance agents; 16 files on the research contract
seeded-defect corpus — 24 variants, balanced 8/8/8 by subtlety, with ground-truth ledger
plugins/crucible/ — the packaged plugin + marketplace entry + experimental plan-mode hook
four agentic-infra hats — prompt-engineer, harness-engineer, skill-designer, eval-engineer
crucible self-review synthesis — the four new hats panel-reviewing crucible itself
manifold markdown XSS fix — scheme allowlist, canonical + re-vendored, 7 new security tests, 378 green
crucible 0.2.0 + manifold 0.1.1 — committed, pushed, and installed from the marketplace

nodes.title: Skills triage — which of the eight deserve work?
nodes.summary: All 8 standalone skills read against the writing-skills criteria. Verdict: the skill bodies are mostly better than feared — the real damage is systemic: discovery (descriptions), testing (zero evals despite a harness existing), and wiring (worktree is orphaned and confirmed Linux-only: GNU sed/find/ln idioms plus bwrap-sandbox assumptions that don't exist on macOS).

nodes.title: Park brief/subset/audit; save worktree, red-vs-blue, hats
nodes.summary: Pi triaged the eight live: three skills he can live without for now, three he uses daily. The keepers get real treatment — worktree fixed by a subagent, red-vs-blue and hats grounded in published research so they 'reference something useful' instead of vibes.

quote.text: I think, uh, brief and subset and audit are things that I can live without for now. I have much, much more use for the worktree skill as well as I have much, much more use for the red versus blue skill and the hat skill.

nodes.title: Ground rule: no commits — committing is Pi's job
nodes.summary: Everything stays uncommitted in the working tree all session; Pi owns commits. Restated even harder at the PROVE handoff ('You don't need to worry about commits, so don't surface that to me. That's my job.'). The rule holds until the SHIP line, where Pi explicitly authorizes a commit+push so the marketplace can serve the session's work.

quote.text: dont make any commits, wait for workflow to finish

nodes.title: Two tracks dispatched; mainline waits on purpose
nodes.summary: The fork: a background agent takes the worktree fix end-to-end while the deep-research harness fans out on adversarial-review and persona literature. Per Pi's rule the mainline holds — no commits, wait for the workflow — rather than starting rewrites on an evidence base that hasn't landed.

nodes.title: Worktree skill fixed, tested, installed
nodes.summary: The fixer registered worktree in the manifest and sync tooling, fixed the hardcoded source path, and patched the confirmed GNU-isms (sed -i, find -printf, ln -sr, bwrap preflight) with portable or platform-guarded code so the Linux box keeps working. Live on this Mac within 40 minutes of dispatch.

nodes.title: Deep-research harness: 108 agents, claims adversarially verified
nodes.summary: The research workflow fanned out web searches, fetched sources, and adversarially verified claims before synthesizing the cited report — the evidence base the two skill rewrites would stand on. Headline: multi-reviewer critique beats single review and self-critique across every benchmark family studied; self-critique is actually worse than a single fresh reviewer.

nodes.title: Rewrites land: methodology doc + both skills rebuilt on evidence
nodes.summary: After a ~5-hour gap (research landed 14:05; Pi returned 18:57) the landing node does the work the fan-out was for: the adversarial-review methodology doc is written with its D1–D11 decision register, red-vs-blue and hats are both rewritten against it, and eval definitions are added so the harness finally covers them. All uncommitted, per the rule.

nodes.title: Designing the crucible
nodes.summary: The rewritten skills immediately provoked architecture questions: agents or skills? one thing or two axes? what models and effort levels? The answers became a named system — crucible — designed in conversation, documented with a mermaid diagram, then built by a two-track fork.

nodes.title: One-word name; hats and stances are different axes; a cheap worthiness gate
nodes.summary: The design rulings: skills and agents are layers, not alternatives (agents pin model+effort; skills carry protocol); hats (lenses) and red-vs-blue (stances) are orthogonal axes that compose — sometimes 'the red versus blue guy' wears a hat; a hat-authoring doc teaches the model to mint quality hats; and red-vs-blue opens with a cheap worthiness check before spending an adversarial pass. Pi wanted one-word names — the system became 'crucible', the hat library 'wardrobe'.

quote.text: So the first thing I want to say is that I don't like the name. I want some one word names that are more apt for this.

nodes.title: Design approved; research and build forked in parallel
nodes.summary: Pi liked the crucible design and proposed the parallelization himself: an opus research orchestrator (fanning out sonnet readers) answers the hat-file contract questions cheaply, while an implementer builds structure plus v0 hat drafts — the mainline integrates when both land, so rework is confined to file contents, not architecture.

nodes.title: Contract research lands — and contradicts the v0 design
nodes.summary: The strongest findings all pushed against v0: identity personas ('you are a security engineer') gained nothing on average across 162 roles × 9 models — identity flavor must be evidenced harm, not decoration; free-prose analysis must precede structured findings; severity needs anchors and evidence-first ordering. Extended the methodology register with D12–D17.

nodes.title: Phase 1+2 built green — the crucible skeleton exists
nodes.summary: Wardrobe + consult + stance agents all in place, 24 skills validating. One correction caught on landing: the implementer skipped effort pins because official plugin agents don't use that key — but this harness documents that agent definitions carry model and reasoning effort, so the pins went back in.

nodes.title: Integration pass: crucible v1 complete, evidence-grounded end to end
nodes.summary: The research findings were applied across the wardrobe and skills — all 16 files rewritten to the research-backed contract: 10 hats as checklist-contracts (one task-coupled Role sentence, operationally-defined failure classes), every validator green. Nothing committed. One open question deferred deliberately: whether 'frontend' splits into ux + frontend-eng — let real usage decide.

nodes.title: Prove it works — then it ships
nodes.summary: Two hours after v1 landed, Pi asked what wrap-up requires. The answer split into a tidy-up, five decisions, and two future phases — and the decisions came back as an autonomy grant that turned verification into the mission: conformance probes, a seeded-defect corpus, a model×effort benchmark, plugin packaging, and finally crucible reviewing itself.

nodes.title: Test-and-approve autonomy; opus pins stand; sync waits for the marketplace repo
nodes.summary: Pi delegated the approval gate itself: test the five draft skills and if they work, they're approved — no waiting. Opus on red-attacker/adjudicator confirmed, read-only toolsets for stance agents confirmed, and install-sync deferred until Pi creates the GitHub marketplace repo. Phases 3+4 to be planned through plan mode.

quote.text: like i just need you to test them and, uh, fucking see if they work or not if they work then they're approved like you don't need to wait for me

nodes.title: Seven blind probes on a seeded artifact — all five skills pass and ship
nodes.summary: An artifact with 5 planted flaws, then the fan-out: red + blue on the security lens, three panelists on the design doc, a worthiness-gate probe, a consult probe — all blind to each other, running while the corpus builder and packaging track started in parallel. Every probe passed its contract; red caught all 5 plants plus 3 real bonus findings; the simplifier panelist flagged a real defect in the test plan itself — the best possible evidence the skill works. All five skills flipped to shipped in the manifest with recorded evidence.

residuals.label: Mid-session agent types don't register
residuals.detail: The first five dispatches used the staged crucible agent types and silently fell through — the harness fixes its agent registry at startup. Re-dispatched as general-purpose with the stance protocol inlined; noted that this fallback path is exactly what a user without the plugin installed hits, so testing it is faithful.

nodes.title: Corpus, packaging, benchmark: recall saturates — the pins stay
nodes.summary: The measurement machinery: a 24-variant seeded-defect corpus with ground-truth ledger, the plugin packaged at plugins/crucible/ with a marketplace entry, and two eval workflows. The 72-agent model×effort benchmark found recall saturated at 100% for every config on both roles — the corpus couldn't discriminate detection tiers; the only signal was false positives on clean artifacts, where sonnet-max was worst (more effort, more false alarms). Pins kept, now with benchmark-grounded rationale. The hat-distinctness eval initially reported perfect lane discipline — v2 of this node records what that result turned out to be worth.
nodes.settled: The pins are retained on evidence: effort doesn't buy recall on this corpus, it buys false positives — and the corpus itself needs a harder v2 before it can discriminate tiers.

residuals.label: Benchmark died in 11ms on launch
residuals.detail: First launch failed instantly (0 agents): workflow args arrived as a JSON string rather than a parsed object, so the configs were undefined. Fixed with defensive parsing and relaunched clean — zero agents had executed, so no compute was wasted.

history.summary: B3 hat-evals verdict: all four hats showed perfect lane discipline (zero out-of-class findings) and beat the no-persona control — distinctness 'verified by eval'.
history.superseded_because: The dogfood panel's eval-engineer showed the instrument was rigged: hat-evals.js instructed reviewers to self-label findings in-class, so zero-out-of-lane was forced, not measured. The honest re-run after the fix kept the conclusion with smaller margins.

nodes.title: Dogfood crucible on itself; mint the missing agentic-infra hats
nodes.summary: Pi's 01:44 return asked two things at once: dogfood the hats, and check whether harness-engineer / prompt-engineer lenses even exist. They didn't — the roster was ten general-software hats. Cleared to proceed: mint four agentic-infrastructure hats (prompt-engineer, harness-engineer, skill-designer, eval-engineer), dry-run their distinctness, then panel them on crucible itself.

quote.text: Dog food the hats and skills to evaluate four dry? Do we have a markdown for a Harness engineer and prompt engineer? Or other harness related stuff?

nodes.title: The recursion: crucible's own panel catches the rigged eval — fix survives the re-run
nodes.summary: Four new hats minted (42–43 lines each, all well-formed), dry-run passed with zero pairwise overlap — then the full recursion: the four hats as blind panelists on the crucible plugin itself. The eval-engineer produced the sharpest result of the session: the distinctness eval had a measurement artifact — reviewers were instructed to self-label in-class, making 'perfect lane discipline' meaningless. Pi ordered a fix methodology; a subagent executed seven fixes (one real crash bug caught in its output: a forbidden shuffle call in the workflow runtime); the corrected instrument re-ran and the distinctness conclusion held. Self-correcting system, demonstrated on itself.
nodes.settled: The hats are real and the instrument is honest: out-of-lane is now measurable rather than rigged to zero, and the four new hats survived their own corrected eval.

residuals.label: Background security review interrupts: manifold markdown XSS
residuals.detail: While the corrected eval ran, an independent background security review flagged a stored-XSS vector in the vendored markdown renderer (unvalidated URL schemes in link hrefs). Verified real, deliberately not fixed mid-task — parked with an explicit note that it must land before foundry goes public. Resolved in the SHIP line.

nodes.title: Leftovers, then the real install
nodes.summary: The post-compaction act: work the queue the compacted summary carried over — the XSS fix, the silent blue-failure gap, the naming overload, and two mechanics questions needing verification — then get crucible actually installed from the marketplace and verified.

nodes.title: Five leftovers settled: XSS fixed, gates added, mechanics verified
nodes.summary: The manifold XSS fixed at the canonical source with a scheme allowlist (javascript:/data:/vbscript: and tab-obfuscated schemes drop to plain text), re-vendored byte-identical, 378 tests green with 7 new security cases. A both-returned gate keeps a silently-dead blue from yielding a clean verdict (parallel guard added to hats); the naming overload pinned with a Terminology block instead of a disproportionate 14-file rename. A background claude-code-guide agent settled the mechanics: effort:/model: pins are honored, and the hook autoloads — with one sharpening: the matcher's tool isn't in the documented matcher list, folded into README, hook comment, and script header.

nodes.title: Push authorized — the no-commits rule retires
nodes.summary: Pi's install ask assumed the work was already on GitHub; investigation showed the opposite — the installed crucible predated the whole session (every fix uncommitted, local HEAD equal to origin). The mismatch was surfaced rather than papered over, and Pi chose 'I commit + push, then verify' from the offered options — a UI-gate answer whose label is the only quotable text. That choice retires the session-long no-commits rule.

nodes.title: Committed, pushed, bumped, installed — and verified from the installed cache
nodes.summary: Precise staging (the unrelated in-progress plugin work excluded), no trailers per Pi's rule, pushed to main. Then the last gotcha: plugin update is version-gated — equal versions skip even when the commit moved — so crucible got a real minor bump to 0.2.0 (4 new hats + fixes) and manifold a 0.1.1 patch (the XSS fix rides to installs). The installed caches verified to contain the fixed builds; the plugin CLI inventories all components including the hook — which narrows the last open question from 'does the hook load' to 'does the matcher fire'.

residuals.label: Voice garble decoded
residuals.detail: Pi's install ask referenced the marketplace by a transcription garble; the registered marketplace resolved to the real GitHub repo and the garble was recognized as voice-transcription noise rather than a missing registration.

nodes.title: Does the plan-mode hook matcher actually fire? Needs a live session
nodes.summary: Everything installable is installed and inventoried; the one thing this session structurally could not verify is behavioral — whether the experimental hook's matcher fires on a real plan-mode exit in a fresh session. Deliberately handed to Pi as the live-check list.
